title: Fire Pit Chair
summary: A low, leaned-back outdoor chair from two 2x4s and two fence pickets — cheap enough per chair to batch-build eight at a time around a fire pit.
description:
Fire pit seating has a math problem: you need a lot of it, and store-bought outdoor chairs times eight is not happening. This design solves it with quantity-friendly materials — two 2x4s, two cedar fence pickets, and a stub of 2x2 per chair — and a geometry that's more relaxed than a dining chair but easier to build than an Adirondack.

The angles are the whole build. The base sides are cut at 10° so the seat tips back; the front legs are beveled at 5° so they sit flat under that tipped base; the back legs carry a 5° bevel at the floor and a 25° bevel at the top where the backrest lands. None of these cuts is hard, but the back legs will test your patience if you cut them one chair at a time.

Which is why the batch tip from the source build is the real gold in this plan: don't cycle the saw between angles per chair. Cut ALL the back legs long with both ends at 5°, then reset the saw once to 25° and come back through the whole stack. The cutting is fast — it's the saw adjustments that eat the afternoon. If you're building eight, batch every cut this way.

Use red-toned or pressure-treated 2x4s for the frame (it lives at ground level, in the weather) and finish with a solid-color penetrating fence stain — it soaks into rough picket faces far better than paint and never peels.
timeLabel: 2.5–4 hrs for the first chair; a weekend for all eight

tools.note: The 5°, 10°, and 25° cuts. Batch every angle across all chairs before touching the saw setting.
Pre-drill the angled 2x2 backrest joint — it wants to split.
Pickets come rough. Sand everything bare skin will touch.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared
materials.note: Seat and back slats. Quantities are per chair — multiply by the batch.

materials.name: 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Red-toned or pressure-treated — the frame sits in the weather at ground level.

materials.name: 2x2, 2 ft
materials.note: Backrest rail. One 8-footer covers four chairs.

materials.name: Solid-color penetrating stain
materials.note: Fence-rated. About a pint per chair; buy a gallon for a batch of eight.

cutList.note: One end cut at 10° off square (angle, not bevel), long point measurement — this tips the seat back.
One end beveled 5°, long point measurement.
5° bevel at the bottom, 25° bevel at the top, long point to short point. Batch-cut: all legs at 5° first, then one saw change to 25°.
Cut all three from the same picket so color and width match.

steps.title: Assemble the seat base
steps.body:
Lay out the base rectangle — front, back, and the two 10°-angled sides — with both angled sides oriented the same way, long point down. Screw together with the 2-1/2" screws, two per joint.

That 10° is the recline of the finished chair. If one side goes in flipped, the base twists and nothing after this step fits.

steps.title: Attach the front legs
steps.body:
Flip the base upside down — it's much easier to register the legs that way — and attach each front leg with three 2-1/2" screws and glue, matching the leg's 5° bevel orientation to the base so the foot will sit flat on the ground.

Glue matters on these joints: legs on outdoor chairs work loose from racking long before the wood fails.

steps.title: Cut and attach the back legs
steps.body:
The back legs carry the fussiest cuts in the build: 5° bevel at the floor, 25° bevel at the top, long point to short point. Batch them — cut every leg at 5° both ends first (23-1/2", ends parallel), then change the saw once to 25° and recut the tops of the whole stack.

Attach with two screws per joint. The 25° tops are what the backrest rail lands on.

steps.title: Fit the backrest rail
steps.body: The 2x2 rail attaches across the back leg tops at the recline angle, which makes it an awkward screw. Pre-drill everything — a 2x2 driven at an angle without a pilot hole splits, full stop. Pocket screws driven from the inside faces are a clean alternative; the seat slats cover them later.

steps.title: Lay the seat slats
steps.body:
Attach the front and back seat slats first with the 1-1/4" screws, then center the middle slat with even gaps.

Gaps aren't cosmetic on outdoor seating — rain has to drain through or the slats cup and rot. Cut all three slats from the same picket so the seat reads as one surface.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

steps.title: Hang the back slats and finish
steps.body:
Set the two outer back slats first, screwed into the backrest rail and the rear seat slat, then space the middle slats evenly and attach.

Sand every edge — pickets throw splinters until you do — and finish with the penetrating stain. Then build the next seven; the second chair goes twice as fast, and by the fourth you'll have the angles memorized.
materials: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72", not dog-eared

images.alt: Low wooden fire pit chairs with slatted seats arranged around a fire pit
